speaker: interviewer
text: December 15, 1995, interview with Sol Urbach, take number seven. Sol?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We're looking at some photos, old photos of my family. On the left side on top is my family, of which all but myself perished during World War II, and all of them perished--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're not talking about the right one. No?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no. We're only doing-- I'm sorry. What do we do now?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Take it back. [INAUDIBLE] Take it back. Erase it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually, we can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: We can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just do the picture on the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So should I continue?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no. No, just this-- the pic--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the picture on the right is my father, who was Akiva Baldinger from Kalwaria Zebrzydowska in Krakow, Poland. Sitting is my-- my aunt, and their two, and two children from Kalwaria Zebrzydowska. And the man on the right is my uncle. And they are all-- the last name is Baldinger, of all them. This picture was probably taken sometimes in-- before World War II. This is how I remember my grandfather, and so I would assume this is right before World War II.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and you had said your father. That is your grandfather, Akiva Baldinger?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandfather, Akiva Baldinger, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This center top picture shows my mother on the left side, my uncle in the uniform of World War I, and my-- his wife during World War I. It probably was taken in-- in the 1918 or '20s. Evidently, my uncle is in the Polish Army or the Austrian-Polish Army. Austria-Hungarian Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: And their names are all Baldingers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Kalwaria. We're looking at a picture that is of my immediate family. This picture was taken pre-World War II, 1937. That's-- that's the last picture of my family that I have. In fact, this picture, I did not have with me during the war. I did not survive with that picture. This picture was made available to me by my uncle and aunt, who lived in the United States from World War-- prior to World War I. In this picture, top left is my older sister, Regina. To her right is my mother, then my father. My mother is Bertha. My father is David. Next to him, standing in the white shirt, is my brother, Samuel, the older brother. Below my older brother in the cap, is myself. Next to me is Haskel, the younger brother of mine. And next to him is Simon, the youngest of the children. And next to him standing, the girl, with the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Necklace?

speaker: interviewee
text: --necklace is Anne. This whole family has perished in the liquidation of the Krakow ghetto. I have never seen them. I have never seen anyone from the day of March 12, 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken someplace in Krakow, in-- in Borek [? Falecki ?] around 1937. This picture is of my mother. This is-- it would appear to be when she was still a single lady and taken in Kalwaria someplace in 1900 and-- potentially '20. This is a-- a little album, a kind of thing a memory little book that was created for me in Brunnlitz in the last camp where we were liberated after the day of liberation. A friend of mine from now last was in Israel, fellow by the name of Max Blazenstein, which was my coworker as a cabinet maker, has made this album. And it depicts a carpenter at work, a prisoner-- a prisoner carpenter at work. And in that album, many of my friends have signed their name and extended the best-- best wishes to me for life in the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of myself. It's one of the earliest pictures after liberation taken in 1945 in Poland, right up on my arrival from Czechoslovakia from liberation. This would be the earliest picture. These are pictures of my-- of Ada and myself taken in 1947 just after we met in Germany, both displaced persons. And this is the time when we began our courtship from then on.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was taken in Baumberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Baumberg in 1947. This is a picture of Ada and myself for the-- during our wedding ceremony when we got married in 1950-- June 3, 1950 in New York City. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By that time, life has made a full turn, and we both were married and began our life as a married couple in New York City.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? I ?] [? thought ?] there are kids there-- [? in ?] [? front ?] [? of ?] [? it. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but I don't know that you're going see--

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of the house my grandfather, Akiva Baldinger, lived in, and his son, two grandchildren, and the son's wife. In Kalwaria Zebrzydowska, this picture would have been taken sometimes in-- in 1973 when we, as a family, visited my grandfather's home. In front of the picture is standing my daughter-in-law, Zipore, my daughter, Barbara, Ada, my wife, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and David and-- and Henry. We're standing right in front of my grandfather's home. Again, we are standing in the groups that went in 1974 to Poland. David, his wife, Barbara, and Henry together with David and Ada, are standing in front of my house in Borek [? Falecki. ?] And this was, again, taken in 1973 on a trip to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does the house look the same as it looked before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: The house-- the house looks identical to the way it looked before the war. In fact, it was not finished when we lived in it, and when we returned 25 years later, it was still not finished. This house had a-- featured a-- a hand-dug well, where you would have to throw in a pail to fetch some water out of it. And it featured an outdoor facility. This house is in-- in Nowy Sacz, where my wife, Ada's, family lived until they have moved from that house to Krakow. This picture was taken on a trip that we made in 1973 to Poland. Ada's family actually continued living there after she moved to Krakow already. So this was a Birnbaum house for a long time. Many of the neighbors that we spoke to there at that time knew that and remembered Ada's parents very well, in fact, and pointed out many of the Jewish neighbors of families and friends that lived in the neighborhood. This is a family photo THAT-- of our family, Ada and my-- my own family, taken in 1993. On the-- starting left, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is Michael Lissner, my-- our son-in-law. Next to him is Barbara Lissner, Barbara Urbach Lissner. Next to Barbara is Ada. Then I am in the middle there. In back of me is our oldest son, David. Next to him is Zipore, his wife. And next on top is Henry, our youngest son. And moving down from Henry, on the right side, is Rebecca, our oldest granddaughter. Next to her is Simone. Rebecca is 12 years old there. Simone is 10. Jonathan is five. Jonathan is in front of his mother, Zipore. And next to Jonathan is Elise. Elise is also five. And next to Elise is Samuel Lissner. And he's also known as [? Punchy. ?]
